title: The Small Delete Register
subtitle: "- — for charwise deletes that wouldn't be worth a numbered slot.
summary: When you make a small charwise delete (less than a line, no newline), the deleted text goes into "-, leaving the numbered registers untouched.

blocks.text: {key:x}, {key:d}{key:w} on a tiny word, {key:r}{c} replacing one character — none of these *should* clobber your last big delete in {key:"}{key:1}. So Vim diverts them to {key:"}{key:-}, the **small delete register**.

blocks.title: What Counts as Small
blocks.text:
A delete goes to {key:"}{key:-} (and *not* into the numbered register chain) when it is:

  • **Charwise** (not linewise or blockwise).
  • Less than one full line.
  • Doesn't contain a newline.

Anything else — {key:d}{key:d}, {key:d}{key:i}{key:p}, {key:d}{key:%} on a multi-line bracket — goes into {key:"}{key:1} and shifts the numbered registers.

The unnamed register {key:"}{key:"} is *still* updated by both small and big deletes. So you can always paste the most recent delete with {key:p}, regardless of which numbered slot it landed in.

The practical upshot: you can {key:x} a typo or {key:d}{key:i}{key:w} a small word freely without losing the line you carefully deleted into {key:"}{key:1} for re-pasting.
The {key:r}{c} replace command writes the *replaced* character into {key:"}{key:-}. Useful occasionally for swap-back-what-I-just-replaced.
